title: A Conversation About Climate Change
summary: Lena and Mark, two friends in Australia, discuss their personal views on climate change, the actions individuals can take, and whether governments or citizens bear more responsibility.

body.speaker: Lena
body.text: Did you read that report about the record temperatures last summer? It was pretty alarming.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: I did. And it's not just temperatures — the bushfires, the flooding, all of it seems to be getting worse every year.

body.speaker: Lena
body.text: It makes me feel quite anxious, honestly. I keep thinking about what kind of world we're leaving for the next generation.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: I understand that feeling. But I also think there are things we can actually do. Have you made any changes to reduce your own carbon footprint?

body.speaker: Lena
body.text: I've started eating less meat, and I've been cycling to work instead of driving. Small things, but they add up, I hope.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: That's great. I switched to solar panels at home last year, and our electricity bills have dropped significantly. It's good for the environment and for the wallet.

body.speaker: Lena
body.text: That sounds like a bigger investment though. Not everyone can afford that.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: That's true. Which is why I think governments need to do more. Tax incentives, subsidies for renewables, tougher regulations on big corporations — those things can't come from individuals alone.

body.speaker: Lena
body.text: I completely agree. Individual actions matter, but they feel like a drop in the ocean compared to what large industries produce.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: Exactly. The top one hundred companies are responsible for something like seventy percent of global emissions. We can't cycle our way out of that problem.

body.speaker: Lena
body.text: So do you think we'll actually see enough political action in time? I'm not sure I'm optimistic.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: I'm cautiously hopeful. The shift to renewable energy is happening faster than most people expected a decade ago. And younger generations are putting real pressure on governments and businesses.

body.speaker: Lena
body.text: That's true. The school climate strikes made a big impact. It's encouraging when you see young people so engaged.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: Absolutely. And technology is advancing quickly too. Electric vehicles, battery storage, green hydrogen — solutions are being developed even if they haven't been fully implemented yet.

body.speaker: Lena
body.text: I suppose the challenge is speed. We have the tools, but we need to move much faster than we have been. Otherwise we'll miss the critical windows for action.

body.speaker: Mark
body.text: Agreed. It'll take individual effort, political will, and serious corporate accountability working together. None of them alone will be enough.

annotations.meaningVi: nhiệt độ kỷ lục, nhiệt độ cao nhất từ trước đến nay
annotations.exampleEn: The country recorded record temperatures during the heatwave last July.

annotations.meaningVi: dấu chân carbon, lượng khí CO₂ thải ra do hoạt động của một người
annotations.exampleEn: Flying less is one of the most effective ways to reduce your carbon footprint.

annotations.meaningVi: tích lũy lại, cộng dồn để tạo ra kết quả đáng kể
annotations.exampleEn: Small savings can add up to a significant amount over a year.
Many households have switched to LED lighting to save energy.

annotations.meaningVi: ưu đãi thuế, các biện pháp giảm thuế để khuyến khích hành vi nhất định
annotations.exampleEn: The government introduced tax incentives to encourage businesses to go green.

annotations.meaningVi: trợ cấp cho năng lượng tái tạo
annotations.exampleEn: Subsidies for renewables have made solar power affordable for many households.

annotations.meaningVi: một giọt nước trong đại dương, quá nhỏ bé so với quy mô vấn đề
annotations.exampleEn: The donation was generous, but it was a drop in the ocean compared to what was needed.
International agreements aim to reduce global emissions by fifty percent by 2050.

annotations.meaningVi: thận trọng nhưng hy vọng, lạc quan có chừng mực
annotations.exampleEn: Scientists are cautiously hopeful that the new treatment will be effective.

annotations.meaningVi: các cuộc đình công khí hậu của học sinh, phong trào biểu tình về khí hậu do học sinh tổ chức
annotations.exampleEn: The school climate strikes inspired millions of young people to demand government action.

annotations.meaningVi: hydro xanh, nhiên liệu hydro sản xuất bằng năng lượng tái tạo
annotations.exampleEn: Green hydrogen is seen as a key fuel for decarbonising heavy industry.

annotations.meaningVi: các cửa sổ hành động quan trọng, khoảng thời gian cần thiết để hành động có hiệu quả
annotations.exampleEn: Scientists warn that we are running out of critical windows for action on climate change.

annotations.meaningVi: ý chí chính trị, quyết tâm của chính phủ để thực hiện thay đổi
annotations.exampleEn: Solving the housing crisis requires both funding and political will.

annotations.meaningVi: trách nhiệm của doanh nghiệp, tính minh bạch và trách nhiệm của các công ty
annotations.exampleEn: Corporate accountability is essential for ensuring that companies meet their environmental commitments.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect — Actions with present relevance
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thì hiện tại hoàn thành được dùng để mô tả những hành động đã xảy ra và vẫn còn liên quan đến hiện tại. Ví dụ: "I've started eating less meat", "I've been cycling to work", "I switched to solar panels last year". Cả "have + past participle" và "have been + -ing" đều xuất hiện ở đây. Dạng tiếp diễn (have been cycling) nhấn mạnh hành động đang diễn ra liên tục, trong khi dạng thường (have started) nhấn mạnh kết quả đã đạt được.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The present perfect describes recent actions that are still relevant now. 'I've started eating less meat' (simple present perfect) emphasises the result — a new habit has been adopted. 'I've been cycling to work' (present perfect continuous) emphasises the ongoing nature of the habit. The difference is subtle but important: continuous forms suggest the action is still in progress.

grammarNotes.title: Modal Verbs — Expressing necessity and possibility
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong bài hội thoại, các động từ khuyết thiếu được dùng để nói về sự cần thiết và khả năng. Ví dụ: "governments need to do more", "those things can't come from individuals alone", "We can't cycle our way out of that problem", "we need to move much faster". 'Need to' diễn đạt sự cần thiết; 'can't' diễn đạt điều không thể thực hiện được. Các động từ khuyết thiếu rất quan trọng trong thảo luận về vấn đề xã hội.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Modal verbs express degrees of necessity, possibility, and ability. 'Need to' indicates something is necessary or required. 'Can't' expresses impossibility. These modals are especially common in opinion-based discussions about social or environmental issues, where speakers want to express strong views without being absolute.

translationVi:
Lena: Bạn có đọc báo cáo về nhiệt độ kỷ lục mùa hè năm ngoái không? Khá đáng lo ngại đó.
Mark: Tôi có đọc. Và không chỉ là nhiệt độ — cháy rừng, lũ lụt, tất cả mọi thứ dường như ngày càng tệ hơn mỗi năm.
Lena: Nó khiến tôi cảm thấy khá lo lắng, thật lòng mà nói. Tôi cứ nghĩ đến thế giới mà chúng ta đang để lại cho thế hệ tiếp theo.
Mark: Tôi hiểu cảm giác đó. Nhưng tôi cũng nghĩ rằng có những điều chúng ta thực sự có thể làm. Bạn có thay đổi gì để giảm dấu chân carbon của mình không?
Lena: Tôi đã bắt đầu ăn ít thịt hơn, và tôi đang đạp xe đến cơ quan thay vì lái xe. Những điều nhỏ nhoi, nhưng tôi hy vọng chúng sẽ tích lũy lại.
Mark: Tốt lắm. Tôi đã chuyển sang dùng tấm pin mặt trời tại nhà năm ngoái, và hóa đơn điện của chúng tôi đã giảm đáng kể. Tốt cho môi trường và cũng tốt cho ví tiền.
Lena: Nghe có vẻ là một khoản đầu tư lớn hơn. Không phải ai cũng có thể đủ khả năng đó.
Mark: Đúng vậy. Đó là lý do tại sao tôi nghĩ các chính phủ cần làm nhiều hơn. Ưu đãi thuế, trợ cấp cho năng lượng tái tạo, các quy định chặt chẽ hơn đối với các tập đoàn lớn — những thứ đó không thể đến từ cá nhân một mình.
Lena: Tôi hoàn toàn đồng ý. Hành động cá nhân có ý nghĩa, nhưng chúng cảm giác như một giọt nước trong đại dương so với những gì các ngành công nghiệp lớn sản xuất.
Mark: Chính xác. Một trăm công ty hàng đầu chịu trách nhiệm cho khoảng bảy mươi phần trăm lượng khí thải toàn cầu. Chúng ta không thể đạp xe để thoát khỏi vấn đề đó.
Lena: Vậy bạn có nghĩ chúng ta sẽ thực sự thấy đủ hành động chính trị kịp thời không? Tôi không chắc mình lạc quan.
Mark: Tôi thận trọng nhưng hy vọng. Sự chuyển đổi sang năng lượng tái tạo đang diễn ra nhanh hơn hầu hết mọi người dự kiến một thập kỷ trước. Và các thế hệ trẻ đang thực sự gây áp lực lên các chính phủ và doanh nghiệp.
Lena: Đúng vậy. Các cuộc đình công khí hậu của học sinh đã tạo ra tác động lớn. Thật khích lệ khi thấy giới trẻ gắn bó đến vậy.
Mark: Hoàn toàn đúng. Và công nghệ cũng đang tiến bộ nhanh chóng. Phương tiện điện, lưu trữ pin, hydro xanh — các giải pháp đang được phát triển dù chúng chưa được triển khai đầy đủ.
Lena: Tôi đoán thách thức là tốc độ. Chúng ta có các công cụ, nhưng cần phải nhanh hơn nhiều so với hiện tại. Nếu không, chúng ta sẽ bỏ lỡ các cửa sổ hành động quan trọng.
Mark: Đồng ý. Cần có nỗ lực cá nhân, ý chí chính trị và trách nhiệm doanh nghiệp nghiêm túc cùng làm việc với nhau. Không có cái nào một mình là đủ.

questions.prompt: What event prompts Lena and Mark's conversation?
options: A report about record temperatures last summer
A local bushfire that affected their neighbourhood
questions.explanation: Lena opens by asking if Mark read 'that report about the record temperatures last summer.'
questions.hint: Think about what Lena mentions in her first sentence.

questions.prompt: What personal changes has Lena made to reduce her carbon footprint?
options: Switching to solar panels and buying an electric car
Reducing meat consumption and cycling to work
Becoming vegetarian and using public transport
Buying organic food and avoiding air travel
questions.explanation: Lena says 'I've started eating less meat, and I've been cycling to work instead of driving.'
questions.hint: Lena mentions two specific changes she has made.

questions.prompt: What change did Mark make at home last year?
options: He switched to an electric car
He started composting his food waste
questions.explanation: Mark says 'I switched to solar panels at home last year.'
questions.hint: Mark mentions something he changed about his home's energy source.

questions.prompt: What is one result of Mark's home energy change?
options: His home produces more energy than it uses
His electricity bills have dropped significantly
His house has increased in value
His neighbours have also switched to the same system
questions.explanation: Mark says 'our electricity bills have dropped significantly.'
questions.hint: Mark mentions a financial benefit alongside the environmental one.

questions.prompt: What does Lena mean when she says individual actions feel 'like a drop in the ocean'?
options: Individual actions are essential and should not be underestimated
Individual actions are too small to matter compared to industrial emissions
Individual actions are only effective near the coast
Individual actions can have a ripple effect on others
questions.explanation: 'A drop in the ocean' is an idiom meaning something is too small to make a significant difference — here contrasting personal action with industrial emissions.
questions.hint: This is a common English idiom about scale.

questions.prompt: According to Mark, approximately what percentage of global emissions are caused by the top one hundred companies?
questions.explanation: Mark says 'The top one hundred companies are responsible for something like seventy percent of global emissions.'
questions.hint: Mark uses this statistic to argue that individuals alone cannot solve the problem.

questions.prompt: How does Mark describe his feelings about the future of climate action?
questions.explanation: Mark says 'I'm cautiously hopeful' — meaning he sees reasons for hope but is careful not to be overconfident.
questions.hint: Mark uses a specific two-word phrase to describe his emotional position.

questions.prompt: What positive development does Mark mention regarding energy?
options: Fossil fuels are becoming cheaper to use
The shift to renewable energy is happening faster than expected
Nuclear power has been accepted as a solution worldwide
Governments have agreed to stop new coal projects
questions.explanation: Mark says 'The shift to renewable energy is happening faster than most people expected a decade ago.'
questions.hint: Mark is talking about a trend that has surprised people.

questions.prompt: Which technologies does Mark mention as emerging climate solutions?
options: Nuclear fusion, wave power, and biofuels
Electric vehicles, battery storage, and green hydrogen
Carbon capture, smart grids, and geothermal power
Solar farms, desalination, and tidal energy
questions.explanation: Mark mentions 'Electric vehicles, battery storage, green hydrogen' as examples of developing solutions.

questions.prompt: According to Lena, what is the main challenge even though solutions exist?
options: The cost of technology is too high for most countries
Scientists disagree about which solutions are best
We need to move much faster than we have been
People are not interested in changing their behaviour
questions.explanation: Lena says 'we have the tools, but we need to move much faster than we have been.'
questions.hint: The issue is about urgency, not the absence of solutions.

questions.prompt: What three things does Mark say must work together to address climate change?
options: Individual effort, political will, and corporate accountability
questions.explanation: Mark concludes: 'It'll take individual effort, political will, and serious corporate accountability working together.'
questions.hint: Mark's final statement summarises his overall view.

cloze.template: Lena: Did you read that report about the record {{b1}} last summer? It was pretty alarming. Mark: And it's not just temperatures — the bushfires, the flooding, all of it seems to be getting worse every year. Lena: It makes me feel quite anxious. I keep thinking about what kind of world we're leaving for the next {{b2}}. Mark: Have you made any changes to reduce your own carbon {{b3}}? Lena: I've started eating less meat, and I've been cycling to work instead of driving. Small things, but they {{b4}}, I hope. Mark: I switched to solar panels at home last year, and our electricity bills have dropped {{b5}}. Lena: Not everyone can afford that. Which is why governments need to do more — tax {{b6}}, subsidies for renewables, tougher regulations on big corporations. Mark: Exactly. The top one hundred companies are responsible for something like seventy percent of global {{b7}}. We can't cycle our way out of that problem. Lena: Do you think we'll actually see enough political action in time? Mark: I'm {{b8}} hopeful. The shift to renewable energy is happening faster than most people expected. And younger generations are putting real {{b9}} on governments and businesses. Lena: The school climate strikes made a big impact. It's encouraging when you see young people so {{b10}}. Mark: It'll take individual effort, political will, and serious corporate {{b11}} working together. None of them alone will be enough.

blanks.explanation: 'Record temperatures' means temperatures that are the highest ever measured — a key indicator of climate change.
'The next generation' refers to the young people who will inherit the world — a common phrase in discussions about long-term sustainability.
'Carbon footprint' is the total amount of greenhouse gases produced by a person's activities — a key term in environmental discussions.
'Add up' means small amounts combine to create a significant total over time — Lena hopes her small actions will collectively make a difference.
'Dropped significantly' means decreased by a notable amount — 'significantly' is a common adverb used with factual statements about measurable change.
'Tax incentives' are reductions or exemptions in taxation designed to encourage specific behaviours, such as investing in clean energy.
'Global emissions' refers to the total amount of greenhouse gases released worldwide — central to climate change discussions.
'Cautiously hopeful' is a set phrase meaning optimistic but careful — the adverb 'cautiously' signals that the hope is guarded and not blind.
'Putting pressure on' is a phrasal collocation meaning to push or force someone to act — commonly used in political and social contexts.
'Engaged' here means actively interested and participating in a cause — 'engaged' is the most natural adjective in this context.
'Corporate accountability' means that companies are held responsible for their actions — a key concept in the climate debate.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mark argues that individual action alone cannot solve the climate crisis because large corporations are responsible for most emissions. Do you agree that governments should focus mainly on regulating corporations rather than asking individuals to change their behaviour? What are the risks of each approach?
